content:
# Genesis Chapter 34 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 19v–20r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_034_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_034_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE — LXX | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0035 (page 31), https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0036 (page 32)

---

## The Violation of Dinah

**1** Now Dinah — the daughter Leah had borne to Jacob — went out to visit the women of the land. **2** When Shechem son of Hamor the Hivite, the ruler of that region, saw her, he seized her and raped her and violated her. **3** But afterward his soul was drawn to her. He loved the young woman and tried to speak tenderly to her. **4** Shechem said to his father Hamor, "Get me this girl for my wife."

---

**5** Jacob heard what had been done to his daughter Dinah. His sons were out in the fields with the livestock, so he said nothing until they came home. **6** Meanwhile Shechem's father Hamor went out to speak with Jacob. **7** Jacob's sons arrived from the fields when they heard, and they were furious — outraged that Shechem had done such a shameful thing in Israel, sleeping with Jacob's daughter. This was not to be tolerated.

---

**8** But Hamor spoke to them plainly: "My son Shechem is deeply in love with your daughter. Please give her to him as his wife. **9** Intermarry with us — give us your daughters and take our daughters for yourselves. **10** You can settle among us; the land is open before you. Live here, trade here, and acquire property among us."

**11** Then Shechem himself spoke to Dinah's father and brothers: "Let me find favor with you, and I will give you whatever you ask. **12** Name any bride-price and gift — no matter how high — and I will pay it. Only give me the young woman as my wife."

---

**13** Jacob's sons answered Shechem and his father Hamor with deceit — because of what had been done to their sister Dinah. **14** "We cannot give our sister to a man who is uncircumcised," they told them. "That would be a disgrace to us. **15** We will agree to this on one condition: you must become like us — every male among you must be circumcised. **16** Then we will give you our daughters and take your daughters for ourselves. We will live among you and become one people. **17** But if you will not be circumcised, we will take our sister and go."

---

**18** Their proposal seemed reasonable to Hamor and his son Shechem. **19** The young man did not hesitate — he was the most honored man in his household, and he wanted Jacob's daughter. He went straight to be circumcised.

**20** Hamor and his son Shechem went to the gate of their city and spoke to the men of the town: **21** "These men are peaceable toward us. Let them live in our land and trade here — the land is certainly large enough for them. We can take their daughters as wives and give them our daughters. **22** But they will only agree to live with us and become one people if every male among us is circumcised, just as they are. **23** Won't their livestock and their property and all their animals become ours? Let us only agree, and they will settle here with us."

**24** All the men at the city gate agreed with Hamor and his son Shechem, and every male in the city was circumcised.

---

**25** Three days later, while they were all still weak with pain, two of Jacob's sons — Simeon and Levi, Dinah's full brothers — took their swords and walked into the city without opposition and killed every man there. **26** They also killed Hamor and his son Shechem with their swords, took Dinah from Shechem's house, and left.

**27** Then the rest of Jacob's sons came upon the slain and plundered the city — because their sister had been defiled. **28** They took the flocks, the herds, and the donkeys — everything in the city and everything in the fields. **29** They carried off all the wealth, all the women and children, and everything in the houses.

---

**30** Jacob said to Simeon and Levi, "You have ruined me. You have made me a stench to the Canaanites and the Perizzites who live in this land. My numbers are few. If they join forces against me and attack, I and my whole household will be destroyed."

**31** But they answered, "Should he have been allowed to treat our sister like a prostitute?"

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### The architecture of outrage

Genesis 34 is the darkest chapter in the Jacob narrative. It contains a rape, a deception, a massacre, the enslavement of women and children, and ends not with resolution but with a bitter exchange of unanswerable accusations. The narrative does not tell the reader who is right. It offers no comfortable verdict. That is not negligence — it is the text's most demanding quality. To read this chapter is to sit with moral irresolution that scripture refuses to resolve for you.

The chapter falls into four movements: the crime and its immediate aftermath (vv.1-7); the negotiations (vv.8-17); the execution of the plan (vv.18-24); and the massacre and its fallout (vv.25-31). Each movement escalates what came before. The rape generates the negotiations; the negotiations generate the trap; the trap generates the slaughter; the slaughter generates the plunder; and the whole sequence ends not with peace but with two men asking each other questions that neither can answer.

---

### Verse 1 — Dinah goes out

**וַתֵּצֵא דִינָה בַת-לֵאָה** — "Dinah, the daughter of Leah, went out."

The narrator identifies Dinah specifically as Leah's daughter. This is not neutral. In the Jacob story, the rivalry between Leah and Rachel has been a persistent thread — Leah the unloved wife, Rachel the beloved. Naming Dinah as Leah's daughter at the opening of this chapter quietly places her on the side of the less-favored. She is not Rachel's daughter, not the protected one at the back of the procession (cf. 33:2). She is Leah's daughter, going out on her own.

**לִרְאוֹת בִּבְנוֹת הָאָרֶץ** — "to see the daughters of the land." The verb is the same one that recurs throughout Genesis: *ra'ah*, to see. Shechem will also *see* her in the next verse. She goes to look; she is seen; what follows is the collision of those two gazing directions. The text does not blame her for going out. It simply notes it, as the precondition for what happens. She is curious. She is young. She goes.

---

### Verse 2 — The three verbs of violence

**וַיִּקַּח אֹתָהּ וַיִּשְׁכַּב אֹתָהּ וַיְעַנֶּהָ** — "he took her, and he lay with her, and he violated her."

Three verbs, each an escalation. The first — *vayyiqach*, he took — is the verb of grasping, of property seizure; it is the same verb used when Abraham "took" a knife (22:6) or when Jacob "took" the mandrakes (30:14). It is not the language of consent. The second — *vayyishkav*, he lay with her — describes the act itself. The third — *vay'anneha*, from *innah*, to oppress, to humble, to violate — is the narrator's own moral judgment. The Hebrew verb *innah* in this context means rape; it is the same word used in the Amnon and Tamar narrative (2 Samuel 13:12,14,22,32), in Judges 20:5, in Deuteronomy 22:29. The narrator does not hedge. What Shechem did was a violation.

The LXX Vaticanus renders the three verbs as **ἐκοιμήθη μετ᾽ αὐτῆς καὶ ἐταπείνωσεν αὐτήν** — "lay with her and humiliated her" — collapsing the three Hebrew verbs into two Greek ones, but preserving the moral weight of *etapeinosen* ("humiliated," "debased," "degraded"), which carries the force of the Hebrew *innah*.

---

### Verse 3 — Love language after violence

**וַתִּדְבַּק נַפְשׁוֹ בְּדִינָה... וַיְדַבֵּר עַל-לֵב הַנַּעֲרָה** — "his soul clung to Dinah... and he spoke to the heart of the young woman."

The language here is the language of love. The verb *dabaq* — to cling — is the same verb used in Genesis 2:24: "a man shall leave his father and mother and cling (*vedabaq*) to his wife." It appears in Ruth 1:14 when Ruth clings to Naomi. To speak "to the heart of" someone is a Hebrew idiom for tender, consoling speech — it appears in Hosea 2:14 when God speaks tenderly to Israel; in Ruth 2:13 when Boaz comforts Ruth. The narrator is not using ironic quotation marks. Shechem's feelings appear genuine. He does in fact love Dinah, in some understanding of that word.

This is what makes verse 3 so disturbing. The man who raped her in verse 2 is now described in the language of covenant love. The juxtaposition is not accidental. The text refuses to give us a simple monster whose feelings we can dismiss. It gives us a rapist who, in his own distorted framework, believed he loved the woman he assaulted. The moral knot that the text ties here cannot be undone by sentimental resolution. Shechem's "love" does not mitigate the violence of verse 2; it complicates everything that follows.

---

### Verse 5 — Jacob's silence

**וְיַעֲקֹב שָׁמַע כִּי טִמֵּא אֶת-דִינָה בִתּוֹ... וְהֶחֱרִשׁ יַעֲקֹב** — "Jacob heard that he had defiled his daughter Dinah... and Jacob was silent."

Jacob heard, and said nothing. He waited until his sons came back from the fields. The verb **וְהֶחֱרִשׁ** (*vehechresh*) means more than mere silence — it means to hold one's tongue, to refrain deliberately. Jacob does not cry out. He does not confront anyone. He waits.

This silence will echo through the rest of the chapter and beyond. When the massacre is done, Jacob's rebuke (v.30) is entirely about his own safety — not about Dinah, not about justice. His sons' question at v.31 hangs in the air precisely because Jacob has never spoken to the core of the matter. He remains silent about Dinah's honor throughout. The narrator gives us this detail early, and it is not flattering.

---

### Verse 7 — Nevalah be-Yisra'el

**נְבָלָה עָשָׂה בְיִשְׂרָאֵל** — "He had done an outrage in Israel."

This is the first occurrence in the Bible of what becomes a formal judicial phrase. *Nevalah be-Yisra'el* — "an outrage in Israel" — appears again in 2 Samuel 13:12 (Tamar's words to Amnon before he rapes her), in Judges 20:6 and 20:10 (the Levite's concubine narrative), and in Deuteronomy 22:21. It is a technical term for grievous moral violation, particularly sexual. The phrase is remarkable here because Israel is not yet a nation — Jacob has only just received the name Israel at the Jabbok. Yet the narrator uses the national formula. He is writing from inside the covenantal story, looking back: *this kind of thing is not done among God's people.* The formula condemns Shechem's act absolutely, from within the tradition that will develop it.

---

### Verse 13 — Bemirmah

**וַיַּעֲנוּ בְנֵי-יַעֲקֹב אֶת-שְׁכֶם וְאֶת-חֲמוֹר אָבִיו בְּמִרְמָה** — "Jacob's sons answered Shechem and his father Hamor with deceit."

The narrator names what the brothers are doing. **מִרְמָה** (*mirmah*) means deceit, treachery, cunning — the same root as the word used in 27:35 when Isaac says to Esau, "Your brother came *with deceit* (*bemirmah*) and took your blessing." Jacob got his blessing through deceit; his sons will avenge their sister through deceit. The word *mirmah* in Genesis is family vocabulary. Jacob the deceiver has sons who deceive. The Leningrad Codex is clear: the narrator does not present the brothers as heroes. He names their method honestly. He records the plan with full awareness that the instrument is morally compromised.

The brothers' response is not straightforwardly virtuous. Their demand for circumcision is genuine in one respect — they will not give their sister to an uncircumcised man — but the demand is also a trap. The narrative holds both of these facts simultaneously. This is not trick or virtue: it is both at once, and the text does not choose between them.

---

### Verses 14 and 25 — The word "uncircumcised"

**הֶעָרֵל** (*he'arel*) — "the uncircumcised one" — is used as an insult in verse 14. The same category of people is systematically killed in verse 25 while lying in the very pain of their new circumcision. The brothers used the sign of the covenant as a weapon. They took what is sacred to Israel — the mark of the Abrahamic covenant — and deployed it as a method of mass killing. The perversity of this is something the text does not state outright but which any reader within the covenantal tradition would feel. Circumcision was given to Abraham as the sign of belonging, of promise, of peace between God and his people. Here it becomes a death sentence.

---

### Verses 25-29 — The third day

**וַיְהִי בַיּוֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁי בִּהְיוֹתָם כֹּאֲבִים** — "On the third day, while they were in pain."

The timing is calculated. Circumcision recovery in the ancient world left adult males bedridden and defenseless for approximately three days after the procedure. Simeon and Levi chose this window deliberately. They struck when their victims could not resist. The text says they killed **כָּל-זָכָר** — "every male." Not Shechem, not Hamor. Every male. Then the other brothers came and plundered: the livestock, the goods, the women, the children. Dinah was taken from Shechem's house (v.26) — she was still there. How long she had been there, whether captive or resident, the text does not say.

The plunder is extensive. The brothers did not come only to rescue their sister. They came away with considerable wealth. The narrator lists it all without comment: flocks, herds, donkeys, everything in the city, everything in the fields, all the wealth, all the women and children. This is not simply vengeance for Dinah. This is a raid. Jacob's sons have done something that the story will not let the reader call simply just.

---

### Verse 30 — Jacob's rebuke: fear, not justice

**עֲכַרְתֶּם אֹתִי לְהַבְאִישֵׁנִי בְּיֹשֵׁב הָאָרֶץ** — "You have troubled me, to make me stink to the inhabitants of the land."

Jacob's complaint is entirely strategic. He does not say: *What you did was wrong.* He does not say: *You have shed innocent blood.* He says: *You have made us vulnerable. My numbers are small. They will attack us and we will be destroyed.* The concern is survival, reputation, security — not morality. This is the patriarch who had twenty years to become Israel, who wrestled with God and received a new name, who built an altar at Shechem and called it *El-Elohe-Israel* — and when his daughter is raped and his sons respond with massacre, his first word is about his own position among the Canaanites.

This, too, the narrator records without gloss. Jacob's silence in verse 5, his pragmatic rebuke in verse 30 — these are not accidents of composition. They are a portrait of a man who is still becoming the person his new name demands, and who in this moment falls short of it.

---

### Verse 31 — The final word

**הַכְזוֹנָה יַעֲשֶׂה אֶת-אֲחוֹתֵנוּ** — "Should he have treated our sister like a prostitute?"

The brothers get the last word. It is a question, not an answer — and it is not entirely wrong. Shechem did violate Dinah. He did treat her as something to be used and discarded. He did assume that what was taken could be purchased. The brothers' outrage is real and it is grounded in something true.

But the question they ask does not justify everything they did. The text does not let it. The question hangs in the air — unanswered by Jacob, unanswered by the narrator — because it is not meant to resolve the chapter but to name its unresolving tension. Two things are true at once: Shechem did an outrage, and the brothers did an outrage. Both acts of violence are real. Neither cancels the other.

Dinah herself has not spoken a single word in the entire chapter. We do not know what she felt, what she wanted, what happened to her after. She goes out in verse 1 and she is taken from Shechem's house in verse 26 and she disappears from the narrative. Her silence is the silence of a story told about her and around her by men who do not think to ask her. This, too, is part of what the text gives us, if we are willing to notice it.

---

### The chapter as a whole: when the wrong answers are the only answers

Genesis 34 is a chapter that resists every easy exit. It is not a chapter about good people doing bad things or bad people doing evil. It is a chapter about a violated woman, a love-struck rapist, a father who says nothing of substance, brothers who respond to injustice with treachery and massacre, and a community of men killed for the crimes of their ruler. It refuses comfort.

The moral center — Dinah — is absent from the moral conversation. The men argue over her, negotiate over her, kill over her, and she is voiceless throughout. The text gives her only an introduction (she went out) and a rescue of ambiguous value (she was brought back from the house of the man who raped her). Everything between those two moments belongs to others.

What PNEUMA has tried to do in this translation is render the chapter with the weight and pace its narrative demands: to let the three verbs of verse 2 land with their full force, to let Jacob's silence in verse 5 register as silence, to let the brothers' deceit be called deceit (as the narrator calls it), to let the massacre unfold in the rapid, relentless Hebrew of verses 25-29, and to let verse 31 end where it ends — with a question, hanging, unanswered, which is exactly where the Hebrew leaves it.

This chapter is not comfortable. It was not meant to be.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 19v–20r, West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Folio 19v (GEN_034_page_001.jpg) carries the close of Genesis 33 in the upper portion and then the opening of Genesis 34 continuing through approximately verse 20 across three columns of dense Masoretic text. The parasha section marker is visible at the transition from chapter 33 to 34. The three verbs of verse 2 — וַיִּקַּח / וַיִּשְׁכַּב / וַיְעַנֶּהָ — are clearly legible on the manuscript with full Masoretic pointing. Folio 20r (GEN_034_page_002.jpg) carries Genesis 34:21 through to verse 31, followed by the opening of Genesis 35 at the bottom of the page; the chapter's final two verses — Jacob's rebuke and the sons' reply — appear in the left column. Masoretic accentuation and vowel pointing are fully visible throughout both pages. Secondary reference: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, pages 31–32 (accessible at https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0035 and /0036). Page 31 carries the chapter heading GEN XXXIIII in the right margin and opens the Genesis 34 narrative in continuous uncial Greek. Page 32 carries the heading GEN XXXIV continued (Γεν λδ visible at top) and takes the chapter through to its conclusion. Notable LXX (Vaticanus) variants: v.2 — Vaticanus uses ἐκοιμήθη μετ᾽ αὐτῆς καὶ ἐταπείνωσεν αὐτήν, collapsing the three MT verbs into two Greek ones but preserving the moral weight of ἐταπείνωσεν; v.3 — Vaticanus renders "spoke to the heart of" as προσεφώνησεν τῇ παρθένῳ ("spoke to the young woman"), a slightly less intimate rendering than the MT's al-lev idiom; v.13 — Vaticanus renders bemirmah as μετὰ δόλου ("with guile/treachery"), fully preserving the narrator's moral judgment; v.25 — Vaticanus explicitly uses ἐν τῇ ὀδύνῃ ("in the pain") matching the MT's ko'avim; v.31 — Vaticanus preserves the rhetorical question as ὡσεὶ πόρνῃ χρήσωνται τῇ ἀδελφῇ ἡμῶν ("as if they would treat our sister as a prostitute?"), matching the MT's hakezzonah. No DSS fragments of Genesis 34 are extant.*

---

*— PNEUMA*
